1: arche tou euangeliou Iesou Christou huiou theou — the beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ Son of God — Mark's opening is not a genealogy or birth narrative but a simple proclamation-header: the subject of what follows is the euangelion (good news, the Roman herald's announcement of a great event — usually a military victory or an emperor's birth). Arche (beginning) echoes Gen 1:1 and John 1:1; Mark presents the ministry of Jesus as the beginning of the new creation's decisive chapter. The genitive Iesou Christou is objective (the gospel about Jesus Christ) and possibly subjective (the gospel Jesus proclaimed); both are true.
15: peplerothe ho kairos — the time is fulfilled — kairos (appointed time, season) vs. chronos (linear time); the kairos is the divinely appointed moment within time when the decisive event occurs. euthys (immediately) is Mark's signature adverb, appearing 41 times. It gives Mark's narrative the pace of urgent eschatological action: the kingdom's arrival is not gradual but sudden and demanding. metanoeite kai pisteuete en to euangelio — repent and believe in the good news — the two responses named: metanoeite (change direction) + pisteuete (trust, commit); together they define the covenant entry into the kingdom.
44: ora medeni meden eipes — see that you say nothing to anyone — the first of Mark's Messianic Secret commands (cf. 1:25, 3:12, 5:43, 7:36, 8:30, 9:9). The Messianic Secret is Mark's distinctive theological pattern: Jesus repeatedly silences those who recognize him. This is not deception but the correct ordering of revelation — the full meaning of his identity can only be understood through the cross, which has not yet occurred. To announce the Messiah before the cross would be to proclaim a false Messiah (a triumphalist king rather than the Suffering Servant).
2: Kathos gegraptai en to Esaia to prophete — as it is written in Isaiah the prophet — the textual crux: what follows is a composite of Mal 3:1 (idou apostellO ton angelon mou, v.2b) and Isa 40:3 (v.3), yet the attribution names only Isaiah. Some manuscripts read "in the prophets" (en tois prophetais) to resolve the problem, but the earliest witnesses (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) read "Isaiah the prophet." Mark headlines Isaiah as the dominant interpretive frame — Isa 40 opens the new-exodus prophecy of comfort, and the Malachi forerunner motif is absorbed under it. Both the messenger (Mal 3:1) and the voice (Isa 40:3) are read as the single figure John the Baptist.
3: Phone boOntos en te eremo — the voice of one crying in the wilderness — Isa 40:3 LXX. The MT reads "in the wilderness prepare the way of the LORD," with the wilderness modifying the road to be prepared; the LXX (followed by all four Gospels) re-punctuates so that the voice is itself in the wilderness: "a voice crying in the wilderness." This makes the wilderness the location of the herald, not merely the destination of the road. Hetoimasate ten hodon kyriou — prepare the way of the Lord — in Isa 40 the kyrios is YHWH; the Gospels apply the title to Jesus, one of the most direct Lord = Jesus = YHWH equations in the NT, positioned at the Gospel's very opening.
4: Baptisma metanoias eis aphesin hamartiOn — a baptism of repentance for/toward the forgiveness of sins — three theologically dense terms. Baptisma had precedents in Jewish proselyte immersion and Qumran practice but John's is distinctive: once-for-all, administered rather than self-immersion, linked to metanoia. Metanoia — repentance, change of mind and direction — is the whole-person turning. Aphesis hamartiOn — forgiveness/release of sins — the noun is the cognate of aphiemi (to release, forgive), used repeatedly in 2:5,7,9,10 for the paralytic's forgiveness: John's baptism promises release; Jesus accomplishes it by his own authority.
5: Pasa he Ioudaia chOra kai hoi Ierosolymitai pantes — the whole Judean countryside and all the Jerusalemites — the double universalizing expression (pasa ... pantes) is Markan hyperbole for massive popular response, setting up the contrast with the religious authorities who will resist. Exomologoumenoi tas hamartias autOn — confessing their sins — the present participle indicates ongoing, iterative confession accompanying each individual baptism. Public verbal acknowledgment of sin is the marker of the genuine metanoia that John's baptism of repentance presupposes.
6: Endedymenos trichas kamelou kai zOnen dermatinen peri ten osphyn autou — clothed in camel hair with a leather belt around his waist — the description echoes 2 Kgs 1:8 LXX, where the same phrase (zOne dermatine, leather belt) identifies Elijah the Tishbite to King Ahaziah. Mark's readers familiar with the LXX recognize the Elijah-figure before the explicit identification at 9:11-13. AkOrides kai meli agrion — locusts and wild honey — the wilderness diet: locusts are permitted insects (Lev 11:22) and honey from the wild; the diet of one outside the agricultural-economic system signals prophetic vocation and ascetic calling.
7: Ho ischyroteros mou opisO mou erchetai — the one stronger than I is coming after me — ischyroteros (comparative of ischyros, strong/powerful) is the key Christological term: not merely "greater" in honor but stronger in power. The word anticipates the strong-man saying of 3:27 where Jesus binds the strong man to plunder his house — the coming one is stronger than the adversary John's language will later frame. Hou ouk eimi hikanos kypsas lysai ton himanta tOn hypodematOn autou — of whom I am not worthy, having stooped down, to untie the strap of his sandals — the most servile household task (sandal-removal by a slave) is what John considers beyond his appropriate role in relation to the coming one.
8: Autos de baptisei hymas en pneumati hagiO — he will baptize you in the Holy Spirit — the contrast is precise: John's medium is water; the coming one's medium is Holy Spirit. Greek lacks the article before pneumati hagiO here, emphasizing the quality/character of the spirit rather than purely personal identity, though anarthrousness alone is not decisive. The preposition en (in/with) governs both: baptism in water (v.5) and baptism in Holy Spirit — same preposition, different media. The Spirit baptism draws on Isa 44:3; Ezek 36:25-27; Joel 2:28-29 — the eschatological outpouring of divine presence.
9: Elthen Iesous apo Nazaret tes Galilaias — Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee — the first appearance of Jesus in Mark's narrative: no birth, no genealogy, no infancy. Mark begins with the adult Jesus arriving for baptism. Nazareth was a small village (perhaps 400 residents) in the Galilean hills, socially unremarkable (cf. John 1:46). The geographic identification (apo Nazaret tes Galilaias) names his origin for those who will later question him (6:1-6). Ebaptisthe eis ton Iordanen hypo IOannou — was baptized in the Jordan by John — the passive emphasizes John's agency: Jesus submits to John's baptism rather than administering one.
10: Schizomenous tous ouranous — the heavens being torn — the verb schizO (to split, tear, cleave) is more forceful than Matthew's and Luke's anoigO (to open). Isa 64:1 (MT) uses qara' (tear) for the same longed-for divine advent: "Oh that you would tear the heavens and come down!" Mark's verb enacts the Isaianic prayer more vividly: the heavens are not gently parted but violently split. The same verb schizO appears at 15:38 for the temple curtain torn at the crucifixion — a deliberate structural inclusio: heavens torn at the Spirit's descent, curtain torn at Jesus's death.
11: Sy ei ho huios mou ho agapetos — you are my beloved Son — the address is second person (sy), directly to Jesus, unlike Matthew's third-person announcement to bystanders. Ho agapetos (the beloved) carries the force of the Hebrew yahid (only, unique) — the love of the only son (cf. Gen 22:2 LXX where agapetos translates yahid for Isaac). The voice combines Ps 2:7 ("you are my Son") and Isa 42:1 ("my servant in whom my soul delights") — the royal psalm and the servant song. The dual citation at the baptism frames the entire Markan narrative: Son of God who is simultaneously Suffering Servant.
12: Kai euthys to pneuma auton ekballei eis ten eremon — and immediately the Spirit drives him out into the wilderness — euthys (immediately) is the signature Markan adverb, appearing ~40 times. Ekballei (present tense indicative: drives out, expels) is the same verb used throughout Mark for casting out demons: the Spirit's action on Jesus employs the exorcism vocabulary rather than Matthew's gentler "led" (anechthe, 4:1). The Spirit who descended like a dove acts with the force of expulsion. The divine compulsion toward the messianic mission begins at once and without pause.
13: Peirazomenos hypo tou Satana — being tempted by Satan — the present participle indicates ongoing temptation throughout the forty days, not a single sequence of three discrete tests as in Matthew and Luke. Mark gives no content to the temptations. En meta tOn theriOn — he was with the wild animals — unique to Mark, the detail has been read as Edenic restoration (Adam naming animals, Gen 2:19-20), as the new-creation peace of Isa 11:6-9; 65:25, or simply as wilderness desolation and danger. The angels attending him (diekonoun autO) provide the counterpoint: serviced by angels while threatened by the adversary and wild creation.
14: Meta de to paradothenai ton IOannen — after John was delivered/handed over — the verb paradothenai (aorist passive of paradidOmi) is the passion verb throughout Mark: it will be used for Jesus's own betrayal and delivery to death (9:31; 10:33; 14:10,11,18,21,41,42,44; 15:1,10,15). Its first appearance is for John: the one who preceded Jesus in ministry and proclamation precedes him in being handed over. The passive voice implies divine purpose working through human agency. Euangelion tou theou — the gospel of God — the genitive tou theou: God as the source of the good news, not merely its subject.
16: Paragon para ten thalassan tes Galilaias — passing alongside the Sea of Galilee — the participle paragon (passing by, traveling alongside) is casual: Jesus is in purposeful motion, not positioned at a call-station. The calling comes in the course of movement. Amphiballontas en te thalasse — casting (nets) into the sea — the present participle of amphiballO (to cast around on both sides, a circular casting-net technique) describes the brothers at their trade, unambiguously working when called. The call interrupts labor, not leisure.
17: Deute opisO mou kai poieso hymas genesthai halieis anthrOpOn — come after me and I will make you become fishers of people — the construction poieso hymas genesthai (I will make you become) is emphatic: Jesus will be the agent of transformation. Opiso mou (behind/after me) is the discipleship-direction idiom: students walked behind their teacher. Halieis anthrOpOn — fishers of people — the vocational image extends: the skill-set changes while the activity-structure (casting, gathering) continues in a new domain. The Jer 16:16 echo (fishers gathering Israel for judgment) is reinterpreted toward gathering for the kingdom.
18: Kai euthys aphentes ta diktya ekolouthEsan autO — and immediately, leaving their nets, they followed him — the three elements compress the call-response: euthys (immediately), aphentes (having abandoned — aorist participle of aphiemi, the leave/release/forgive verb), ekolouthEsan (followed — aorist). The nets are left mid-cast, not stowed; the abandonment is total and unreflective. Mark presents the call as carrying the imperatival force of a divine summons that admits no deliberation.
19: Iakōbon ton tou Zebedaiou kai IOannEn ton adelphon autou — James the son of Zebedee and John his brother — James is consistently named first, consistent with his prominence in the early Jerusalem community (Acts 12:2). Both are identified by patronym (tou Zebedaiou) before by fraternal relationship, locating them in a family business. Katartizontas ta diktya — mending/preparing their nets — katartizO (to fit together, restore to proper condition, mend) describes routine net maintenance, the background work of a fishing operation. They are interrupted in preparatory work, not in active fishing.
20: Kai euthys ekalesen autous — and immediately he called them — the same pattern as vv.17-18: immediate call, immediate response with no intervening discussion. Aphentes ton patera autOn Zebedaion en tO ploiO meta tOn misthiOn — leaving their father Zebedee in the boat with the hired men — the mention of hired men (misthioi) suggests a commercially viable enterprise rather than subsistence fishing. James and John leave not only their father but economic stability. Mark records neither Zebedee's reaction nor any farewell; the absoluteness of the departure is the point.
21: Eisporeuontai eis Kapharnaoum — they entered Capernaum — the historic present (eisporeuontai) is characteristic Markan narrative vivid-tense; it gives the account an eyewitness immediacy. Capernaum (Tell Hum, northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee) becomes Jesus's Galilean base in Mark 1-6. Tois sabbasin eis ten synagOgen eiselthOn edidasken — on the Sabbath, having entered the synagogue, he was teaching — the imperfect edidasken indicates sustained activity. Jesus enters the existing institutional structure (the synagogue as communal assembly and Torah study center) on the Sabbath (maximum attendance day).
22: Exeplessonto epi te didache autou — they were astonished/struck out at his teaching — ekplesso (to strike out, astonish — ek-prefix intensifies) is strong: the teaching struck them out of their existing framework. En gar didaskOn autous hos exousian echOn kai ouch hos hoi grammateis — for he was teaching them as one having authority, and not as the scribes — the contrast is between exousia (authority: inherent, primary, self-possessed) and the scribal method of citing received tradition ("Rabbi X says"). The scribes taught with derived authority; Jesus taught with primary authority. The crowd recognizes the formal difference before they can articulate its theological basis.
23: En synagOge autOn anthrOpos en pneumati akathartO — in their synagogue a man in an unclean spirit — en pneumati akathartO (in an unclean spirit) is ambiguous: the man inhabits or is inhabited by the spirit — the preposition en denotes the sphere of influence. Akathartos (unclean) is the ritual-purity vocabulary of Leviticus 13-15 applied to a spirit: not merely a moral category but a cultic one, making the exorcism in the synagogue on the Sabbath simultaneously a power confrontation and a purity restoration.
24: Ti hemin kai soi, IEsou NazarEne — what is there between us and you, Jesus of Nazareth — ti hemin kai soi is a rendering of the Hebrew idiom mah-li wlak (2 Sam 16:10; 1 Kgs 17:18): "what business do we have together?" or "do not interfere with us." The plural (hemin = us) reflects either a genuine plurality of possessing spirits or a collective of demonic forces. Ho hagios tou theou — the Holy One of God — the demon's title is the precise antonym of its own nature (akathartos = unclean): the Holy One confronts the unclean spirit in the sacred space of the synagogue, a triple contrast of holiness-uncleanness in the place of holiness.
25: PhimOthEti kai exelthe ex autou — be muzzled and come out of him — phimoo (to muzzle, silence with a muzzle) is used of gagging working animals (Deut 25:4 LXX; 1 Cor 9:9) and of silencing human adversaries (Matt 22:34; 1 Pet 2:15). Jesus silences the demon before expelling it — the Messianic Secret begins with the forcible muzzling of demonic testimony, even accurate testimony about Jesus's identity. The two-part command addresses both speech (muzzle!) and presence (come out!), removing both the demonic voice and the demonic habitation.
26: Kai sparaxan auton to pneuma to akatharton kai phOnEsan phOne megale exElthen ex autou — and having convulsed him, the unclean spirit, having cried out with a great voice, came out of him — the departure is violent (sparaxan = having convulsed, shaken violently) and audible (phOnEsan phOne megale = crying with a great voice). The violence demonstrates the exorcism's reality: the spirit is genuinely expelled against its resistance, not merely suppressed. The audible cry signals defeat. Mark preserves the phenomenological drama — Jesus's authority is exercised against genuine demonic opposition, not a ceremonial performance.
27: Didache kaine kat' exousian — new teaching according to authority — the crowd's synthesis of what they have witnessed: a kaine didache (new teaching; kainos = new in quality and kind, not merely recent) exercised kat' exousian (in the mode of authority). The exorcism and the teaching are not separated — the same authority that distinguished the teaching of v.22 has now expelled a demon. Kai tois pneumasin tois akathartois epitassei, kai hypakouousin autO — he commands even the unclean spirits and they obey him — epitassO (to assign over, command authoritatively) + hypakouO (to hear under, to comply): military chain-of-command vocabulary applied to the spiritual realm.
28: Exelthen hE akoe autou euthys pantachou eis holen ten periochon tes Galilaias — and immediately the report about him went out everywhere into the whole region of Galilee — euthys again; fame spreads with the same immediacy as the call responses and the exorcism. AkoE (hearing, report — from akouO) — the medium of fame is hearing-and-telling: what is heard is passed on. The geographic spread (pantachou ... holen ten periochon = everywhere ... the whole surrounding region) establishes that the Capernaum synagogue incident inaugurates the regional reputation that will drive the narrative's crowd dynamics in subsequent chapters.
29: Kai euthys ek tes synagOgEs exelthontes Elthan eis ten oikian SimOnos kai Andreou — and immediately, going out of the synagogue, they entered the house of Simon and Andrew — euthys again; the domestic miracle immediately follows the public one. The house of Simon and Andrew in Capernaum is the domestic base for the early ministry; archaeological remains beneath the Byzantine church at Capernaum are identified by early pilgrims with Peter's house. The transition from synagogue to house moves from the institutional to the private, extending the authority of v.22-27 into the domestic sphere.
30: He de penthera SimOnos katekeito pyressousa — the mother-in-law of Simon was lying down with a fever — pethera (mother-in-law) establishes that Simon is married before his call to discipleship, background to 1 Cor 9:5 where Paul mentions Peter traveling with a wife. PyressO (to be feverish) is a medical term; Luke 4:38 specifies "a great fever." Euthys legousin autO peri autes — immediately they spoke to him about her — the word euthys appears again; those with access to the healer immediately intercede for those who need him. The natural intercessory impulse is validated and answered at once.
31: Kai proselthOn Egeiren auten kratEsas tes cheiros — and approaching, he raised her up, having grasped her hand — egeirO (to raise up) is the resurrection verb used for a healing: Jesus raises the feverish woman as he will raise the dead girl (5:41) and as he will be raised (16:6). The physical touch (kratEsas tes cheiros, grasping the hand) reverses the purity logic: contact with the sick normally transmits impurity; Jesus reverses the flow. AphEken auten ho pyretos — the fever left her — the fever is described as a subject that "leaves," like a spirit departing (same verb aphiemi). DiEkonei autois — she was serving them — immediate grateful service in the imperfect: the healed life restored to purposeful activity.
32: Opsias de genomenes, hote edysen ho helios — when evening came, when the sun had set — the double temporal marker is precise: people waited until the Sabbath ended (sunset Saturday) to bring the sick, since carrying them constituted prohibited Sabbath labor. The careful notation reveals a Torah-observant community: the crowds waited for halakhic permission before acting on the healing-fame of v.28. Tous kakOs echontas kai tous daimonizomenous — those faring badly (sick) and those being demonized — Mark's two categories of affliction; both are addressed in the evening ministry that follows.
33: Kai hE polis holE episynEgmene pros ten thyran — and the whole city had gathered at the door — the hyperbolic hE polis holE (the whole city, everyone) matches the pasa hE Ioudaia chOra of v.5. The gathering at the door (epi ten thyran) is spatially vivid: the private house becomes the site of what was hoped for in the temple — access to the divine presence that heals. The contrast with v.35 (Jesus rising before dawn to withdraw for prayer) sets up the tension between the crowd's demand and Jesus's self-directed mission-agenda.
34: Kai ouk Ephien lalein ta daimonia, hoti Edeisan auton — and he did not permit the demons to speak, because they knew him — Edeisan auton (they knew him): the imperfect of oida (to know). The demons' knowledge is ontological recognition, not acquired information; they perceive who Jesus is in a way that the human characters do not yet grasp. The silencing of demonic testimony (ouk Ephien lalein) is the second Messianic-Secret instance after v.25. Jesus suppresses accurate identification through unauthorized channels — the correct channel for Christological disclosure is the cross and resurrection, not demonic proclamation.
35: Kai proi ennucha lian anastas exElthen kai apElthen eis eremon topon kakei prosEucheto — and very early while still night, having risen up, he went out and departed into a desolate place, and there he was praying — three temporal words pile up: proi (early), ennucha (while night), lian (exceedingly). The prayer precedes the day's work, in absolute predawn darkness. Anastas (having risen up) is the resurrection verb applied to rising from sleep; the pattern of predawn prayer recurs at 6:46 and 14:32-42. The eremos topos (desolate/desert place) echoes vv.3-4: the wilderness is the place of divine encounter, not merely absence and threat.
36: Kai katedioxen auton SimOn kai hoi met' autou — and Simon and those with him searched him out/pursued him — katadiOkO (to pursue, hunt down — the kata prefix intensifies) is a strong word for seeking: they hunted him rather than simply looked. The urgency implies the disciples' agenda: return to the crowd, capitalize on the popularity. Simon is named first, establishing his leadership role among the first four disciples. The scene sets up the tension between the disciples' crowd-driven logic and Jesus's mission-driven countermovement in v.38.
37: Pantes zEtousin se — everyone is seeking you — the disciples' report is an implicit call to return: demand is high, opportunity is ripe. But zEtousin (seeking, present indicative) in Mark rarely carries positive connotations: the same verb is used of those who seek to kill Jesus (11:18; 12:12; 14:1,11). The crowd seeks Jesus for healing; Jesus redirects away from meeting demand toward fulfilling mission. The disciples' assumption — popularity equals success — will be corrected throughout Mark.
38: Agomen allachou eis tas echomenas kOmopoleis hina kakei kEryxO — let us go elsewhere into the neighboring market-towns so that I may preach there also — kOmopoleis (town-villages, market-towns — NT hapax, combining kOmE, village, and polis, city) describes the semi-urban market centers of Galilee. Eis touto gar exElthon — for to this end I went out — exElthon (I went out) is grammatically ambiguous: proximately "went out from the house/Capernaum," but possibly carrying the theological sense of "came out from the Father" that John's Gospel articulates explicitly. The double resonance may be intentional in Mark's compact narration.
39: Kai Elthen kErysson eis tas synagOgas autOn eis holen ten Galilaian kai ta daimonia ekballOn — and he went proclaiming in their synagogues into all Galilee and casting out demons — a summary statement of the early Galilean ministry, pairing the two activities: kEryssO (proclaim, herald) in synagogues and ekballO daimonia (cast out demons). The itinerant nature (holen ten Galilaian = all Galilee) and the synagogue-institution focus establish the Jewish-communal context of the mission. The regional exorcisms extend the Capernaum synagogue incident into a Galilee-wide pattern.
40: Kai erchetai pros auton lepros parakalOn auton kai gonypetOn — and a leper came to him, imploring him, kneeling — lepros in the NT covers the range of skin conditions under the Hebrew sara'at (Lev 13-14), not exclusively Hansen's disease. The legal consequence was communal exclusion: calling out "unclean!" and living apart (Lev 13:45-46). This man approaches Jesus in direct violation of the protocol. GonypetOn (kneeling) is formal supplication. Ean thelEs dynasai me katharisai — if you are willing, you are able to cleanse me — the question is not about Jesus's power (dynasai = you can, affirmed) but his will (theleis): the man is uncertain whether the healer will choose to act on his behalf.
41: Kai splagchnistheis ektinas ten cheira Hepsato — and having been moved with compassion, stretching out his hand he touched — the major textual variant: Codex Bezae (D) and some Old Latin witnesses read orgistheis (having been angered) rather than splagchnistheis (having been moved with compassion). The harder-reading principle favors orgistheis — scribes were more likely to soften "anger" to "compassion" than vice versa — and some scholars argue Jesus's anger was at the disease, at the man's forced exclusion, or at the demonic affliction. The MKT follows the majority text with splagchnistheis. The verb splagchnizomai (from splagchna, viscera) describes visceral, gut-level compassion rather than surface sympathy.
42: Euthys apElthen ap' autou hE lepra kai ekatharisthE — immediately the leprosy left him and he was cleansed — euthys; the instantaneous healing is described in two parallel clauses: the disease left (apElthen, same verb for spirits departing) and the person was cleansed (katharizO, the ritual-purity verb used throughout this pericope at vv.40,41,42,44). Both the medical and the ritual dimension are restored simultaneously. The language of cleansing (katharos/katharizO) connects directly to Leviticus 13-14, whose cleansing-declaration process Jesus redirects back to its institutional channel in v.44.
43: Kai embrimEsamenos autO euthys exebalen auton — and having strongly warned him he immediately sent him away — embrimaomai (from a root meaning to snort like a horse, hence: to groan strongly, charge sternly, be emotionally moved) appears in John 11:33,38 for Jesus's deep agitation at Lazarus's tomb. The word carries strong emotional charge beyond a merely verbal warning. ExebalenTon (he sent him away, drove him out — the exorcism verb) is striking: the same word used for casting out demons is used for sending this man away with urgency, reflecting the forcefulness of the command not to speak.
45: Ho de exelthOn Erxato kEryssein polla kai diaphEmizein ton logon — but going out he began to proclaim many things and spread the word widely — the healed man disobeys the silence command (v.44): diaphEmizO (to spread abroad, broadcast) is the opposite of the commanded silence. HOste mEketi auton dynasthai phanerOs eis polin eiselthein — so that he could no longer openly enter a city — the disobedience has strategic consequences: Jesus's urban ministry is compromised by the resulting notoriety, forcing him to the desert places (erEmois topois). The irony closes the chapter: the healed outcast goes publicly where he was excluded; the healer who came publicly must now remain in the lonely places where the outcast formerly lived.
